title: Mangulam Cave Inscription of Nedunjeliyan I
historical_significance: Earliest epigraphic attestation of a Pandya king and among the oldest Tamil-Brahmi records, bridging Sangam literature with archaeology.
description: The longest Mangulam (Ovamalai) record: Pandya king Nedunjeliyan I (Neduncheliyan) grants a monastery to the senior Jain monk Nanta-siri Kuvan. Among the earliest Tamil-Brahmi texts; preserves early use of the letter ழ.

title: Mangulam Cave Inscription of Katalan Valuti
historical_significance: Documents Jain patronage by officials of the early Pandya court, illustrating how rock-cut monasteries were endowed in the pre-Christian Tamil country.
description: Records that Katalan Valuti, an official (panan) of Nedunjeliyan, caused a rock-cut bed to be made for the Jain monk Nanta-siri Kuvan. Part of the six Tamil-Brahmi inscriptions on Kalinjamalai near Mangulam village.

title: Mangulam Cave Inscription of the Velarai Merchant Guild
historical_significance: Shows merchant guilds (nagarattu) participating in religious endowments alongside royal patrons in early South India.
description: Mentions Antai Asutan, superintendent of pearls and kavidi of the merchant guild of Velarai (Vellarai/Vellarippatti), who donated a drip-ledge to Nanta-siri Kuvan. Shows Jain patronage by traders linked to the early Pandyas.

title: Jambai Cave Inscription of Atiyan Neduman Anji
location: Jambai, Tirukkoyilur taluk, Viluppuram district, Tamil Nadu
historical_significance: Conclusively identifies Emperor Ashoka's Satyaputras with the Athiyaman chieftains, a landmark in South Indian historiography.
description: Reads: Satiyaputo Atiyan Netuman Anci itta Pali — 'the abode was given by Atiyan Neduman Anji, the Satyaputra.' Identifies Ashoka's Satyaputras with the Athiyaman chieftains of Tagadur (Dharmapuri); the donor appears in Sangam anthologies.

title: Pugalur (Arunattarmalai) Tamil-Brahmi Inscription
location: Arunattarmalai, Velayuthampalayam (Pugalur), near Karur, Tamil Nadu
historical_significance: Provides a three-generation Chera genealogy in stone, linking Sangam-era Chera rulers to the archaeological record near ancient Karur (Vanji).
description: Records three generations of Chera rulers — Athan Cheral Irumporai, Perum Kadungon, and Kadungon Ilam Kadungo — and the cutting of a rock shelter for the Jain monk Chenkayapan at the prince's investiture. Key epigraphic link between Sangam Cheras and archaeology.

title: Tamil-Brahmi Cave Inscription (Arittapatti)
location: Arittapatti, Melur taluk, Madurai district, Tamil Nadu
historical_significance: Representative early Jain cave-bed donation in the Madurai region, with script comparable to the Mangulam corpus.
description: Jain cave-bed donation by Imayavan, son of Ilam Perathan of Ilanchi, and by Adhinan Veliyan of Nelveli. Representative Tamil-Brahmi cave epigraphy of the early historic period, with orthography comparable to Mangulam.

title: Tamil-Brahmi Cave Inscription (Tiruchirapalli Rockfort)
historical_significance: Part of the widespread Tamil-Brahmi cave corpus documenting Jain monastic sites before the classical Sangam period.
description: Early Jain cave inscription on the Tiruchirapalli rock, among the corpus of Tamil-Brahmi cave records that document monastic residences (pali) and stone beds in natural caverns before the Sangam classical age.

title: Velvikudi Grant (Copper Plates)
historical_significance: Key Pandya record of post-Kalabhra restoration and one of the earliest documented brahmadeya land grants in the Tamil region.
description: Bilingual copper-plate sasana of Pandya king Nedunjadaiyan Varaguna-varman I (Jatila Parantaka). Restores the brahmadeya village Velvikudi to Brahmana families after the Kalabhra interregnum; narrates Pandya revival and early medieval land grants.

title: Leiden Grant to Chulamanivarma Vihara (Nagapattinam)
location: Nagapattinam, Tamil Nadu (plates preserved in Leiden)
historical_significance: Documents Chola patronage of a Buddhist vihara founded by a Sailendra king, evidencing Nagapattinam's role in Indian Ocean trade networks.
description: Royal endowment of the Buddhist Chulamanivarmavihara at Nagapattinam by Sailendra king Chulamanivarman, confirmed under Chola patronage. Forms the core of the Leiden copper-plate corpus documenting Chola–Southeast Asian maritime relations.

title: Larger Leiden Plates
historical_significance: Twenty-one copper plates that were for decades a primary authentic source for reconstructing early Chola political history.
description: Twenty-one large copper plates recording Rajendra Chola I's inscription of Rajaraja I's grant to the Nagapattinam vihara: five Sanskrit plates (eulogy and genealogy) and sixteen Tamil plates (village revenues, exemptions, and privileges to the Buddhist sangha).

title: Smaller Leiden Plates
historical_significance: Records Kulottunga I's renewal of monastery revenues after turbulent decades of Chola intervention in Southeast Asia.
description: Three Tamil copper plates issued by Kulottunga Chola I, supplementing the Larger Leiden grant. Restores and clarifies revenue rights to the Nagapattinam monastery (Rajarajaperumpalli) after decades of Chola campaigns in the Malay world.

title: Thiruvalangadu Copper Plates of Rajendra Chola I
historical_significance: Contains the most extensive surviving Chola genealogical list, from mythical solar ancestors through Karikala to Rajendra Chola I.
description: Thirty-one plates granting Palaiyanur to the Thiruvalangadu Siva temple. The Sanskrit section preserves the legendary and historical Chola genealogy (from the solar line through Karikala to Rajendra); among the most important sources for early Chola history.

title: Uttiramerur Inscription on Village Assembly (Kudavolai)
date: 919 CE (12th regnal year of Parantaka I)
historical_significance: Classic epigraphic constitution for medieval village self-government, including the kudavolai (pot-ticket) electoral procedure.
description: On the walls of the Vaikunda Perumal temple sabha-mandapa: prescribes election of village committees (variyams) from thirty wards by the kudavolai (pot-ticket) system, with qualifications, disqualifications, and duties — a classic record of medieval local self-governance.

title: Uttiramerur Inscription (Amendment of 921 CE)
date: 921 CE (14th regnal year of Parantaka I)
historical_significance: Amends the 919 CE Uttiramerur rules, showing how village administrative codes were revised in practice.
description: Companion inscription to the 919 CE Uttiramerur record, modifying rules for the annual, garden (totta), tank (eri), and other variyams of Uttaramerur Chaturvedimangalam to make committee administration more practical.

title: Anuradhapura Tamil Householders' Terrace Inscription
historical_significance: Rare pre-Christian evidence of a self-identified Tamil (Dameda) merchant and mariner community in ancient Sri Lanka.
description: Records a terrace (pasada) built for Tamil householders (Dameda-gahapati) by a Tamil monk (Dameda-samana); one seat was reserved for Karava the mariner (navika). Rare pre-Christian evidence of a Tamil merchant and mariner community in ancient Sri Lanka.

title: Koneswaram Inscription (Nilaveli Record)
historical_significance: Links the Koneswaram shrine at Trincomalee to Chola imperial patronage and land endowments in Sri Lanka.
description: Nilaveli slab inscription in mixed Grantha and Tamil granting 250 veli of land to Maccesvaram at Tirukonamalai for the temple's daily expenses. The site also yields Pallava-period Grantha epigraphs (c. 7th century) and a later Rajaraja I prasasti, marking Koneswaram as a major Saiva centre in Sri Lanka.

title: Sittanavasal Jain Cave Tamil-Brahmi Inscription
historical_significance: Among the earliest Jain cave records in the Pudukkottai region, attesting monastic residence before the celebrated medieval frescoes of the same site.
description: Tamil-Brahmi inscription in the Sittanavasal rock-cut Jain beds cave, recording patronage of a monastic shelter (pali). The site later became a major Jain and artistic centre; the epigraph belongs to the early historic layer of the complex.

title: Alagarmalai Tamil-Brahmi Cave Inscription
historical_significance: Documents Jain monastic patronage in the hills near Madurai during the formative period of Tamil literacy and Sangam-age polities.
description: Rock-cut Jain cave inscription on Alagarmalai near Madurai, among the corpus of early Tamil-Brahmi records that name donors and monks associated with natural caverns converted into palli (monasteries).

title: Kodumanal Tamil-Brahmi Potsherd Inscriptions
historical_significance: Hundreds of inscribed potsherds from a major Chera industrial and gem-cutting centre corroborate Sangam references to Kodumanam and Roman-period trade.
description: Excavations at Kodumanal on the ancient Karur–Musiri trade route yielded one of the largest clusters of Tamil-Brahmi inscribed potsherds in India, naming artisans, lapidaries, and traders linked to iron, steel, and gemstone production in the Chera country.

title: Edakal Hill Tamil-Brahmi Inscription
historical_significance: One of the earliest Tamil-Brahmi records in the Kerala–Kongu region, extending the geographic range of early Tamil epigraphy into the western ghats.
description: Tamil-Brahmi inscription in the Edakal cave complex of Wayanad, associated with early historic communities on the fringes of the Chera cultural sphere and long-distance routes toward the Malabar coast.

title: Kallupatti Jain Cave Tamil-Brahmi Inscription
historical_significance: Represents the dense network of Jain cave shelters around Madurai that underpins our understanding of pre-Sangam religious patronage in the Pandya heartland.
description: Tamil-Brahmi record from a Jain cave at Kallupatti near Madurai, documenting rock-cut beds and drip-ledges donated to ascetics — part of the extensive early historic cave epigraphy of the Pandya country.

title: Vallam Cave Inscription of Nedunjeliyan II
historical_significance: Links a later Sangam-period Pandya ruler to a cave endowment and helps correlate literary dynastic lists with epigraphic chronology.
description: Tamil-Brahmi cave inscription associated with Pandya king Nedunjeliyan II (Neduncheliyan), recording provision of a monastic shelter. The Vallam record is cited in studies connecting Sangam anthologies with early Tamil epigraphy.

title: Arachalur (Kodinmalai) Tamil-Brahmi Cave Inscription
historical_significance: Extends the Tamil-Brahmi cave corpus into the Kongu region, an area later central to Chera trade and craft production.
description: Jain cave-bed inscription on Kodinmalai near Arachalur in the Kongu country, naming donors who endowed rock shelters for monks. Illustrates the spread of Tamil-Brahmi literacy beyond the core river valleys.

title: Keelavalavu Tamil-Brahmi Cave Inscription
historical_significance: Adds to the cluster of Madurai-district cave inscriptions that document Jain monasticism among early Tamil chiefdoms.
description: Tamil-Brahmi inscription at the Keelavalavu Jain cave site near Melur, recording donation of a rock-cut bed or shelter. Part of the regional cave epigraphy that predates the classical Sangam corpus.

title: Larger Sinnamanur Pandya Copper Plates
date: c. 10th century CE (Rajasimha II, regnal year 16)
historical_significance: Major Pandya genealogical charter pairing Sanskrit and Tamil praśasti, essential for reconstructing post-Kalabhra Pandya succession.
description: Seven-plate copper grant of Pandya king Rajasimha II (Maravarman Rajasimha II), discovered at Sinnamanur. The bilingual record lists Pandya ancestors from Kadungon onward and forms a cornerstone of medieval Pandya chronology alongside the Velvikudi grant.

title: Smaller Sinnamanur Pandya Copper Plates
historical_significance: Companion Sinnamanur grant of an earlier Pandya ruler, cross-checked with Velvikudi and Aivarmalai records to build a continuous Pandya genealogy.
description: Smaller set of Sinnamanur copper plates issued by a 9th-century Pandya king (Srimara Srivallabha's line). Together with the larger Sinnamanur and Velvikudi grants, it supplies overlapping royal lists used by epigraphists to date Pandya reigns.

title: Aivarmalai Pandya Inscription of Varagunavarman II
historical_significance: Dated Pandya record of Varagunavarman II used as a chronological anchor for 9th-century Pandya–Ganga–Pallava conflicts.
description: Stone inscription of Pandya king Varagunavarman II (Varaguna II) from Aivarmalai, dated to 862 CE. Commemorates military and political events of the later Pandya revival and is cited alongside copper-plate grants in Pandya genealogical studies.

title: Dalavayapuram Pandya Copper Plates
historical_significance: Follows the bilingual Pandya praśasti model of Velvikudi and Sinnamanur, reinforcing the elevation of Tamil in royal charters.
description: Pandya copper-plate grant from Dalavayapuram with Sanskrit and Tamil sections, recording a village endowment and royal genealogy. Represents the mature medieval Pandya epigraphic tradition of the tenth century.

title: Muziris (Pattanam) Tamil-Brahmi Merchant Mark
historical_significance: Archaeological Tamil-Brahmi from the celebrated Chera port Muziris corroborates Greco-Roman and Sangam accounts of Indo-Mediterranean commerce.
description: Tamil-Brahmi inscribed pottery and related marks recovered from excavations at Pattanam, identified with the Chera emporium Muziris (Muchiri) praised in Sangam poetry and Periplus of the Erythraean Sea.

title: Thanjavur Brihadeeswarar Temple Foundation Inscription
historical_significance: Commemorates Rajaraja Chola I's consecration of the Brihadeeswarar temple and records vast endowments that defined imperial Chola temple-state administration.
description: Foundation inscription of the Rajarajesvaram (Brihadeeswarar) temple at Thanjavur, dated to 1010 CE. Lists extensive gifts of gold, silver, land, and personnel to the great Siva temple built in Rajaraja I's 25th regnal year.

title: Gangaikonda Cholapuram Temple Foundation Inscription
location: Gangaikonda Cholapuram, Ariyalur district, Tamil Nadu
historical_significance: Marks Rajendra Chola I's new capital after the Ganges campaign and mirrors the Thanjavur model of imperial Saiva patronage.
description: Inscription commemorating Rajendra Chola I's establishment of Gangaikonda Cholapuram and consecration of the Brihadeeswarar (Gangaikondacholisvaram) temple, linking the capital to his northern expedition and imperial ideology.

title: Karandai Copper Plates of Rajendra Chola I
historical_significance: One of four major Rajendra Chola plate sets preserving conquest lists and temple grants from the height of the maritime empire.
description: Copper-plate grant of Rajendra Chola I with Sanskrit praśasti and Tamil dispositive portions. Records endowments and includes royal eulogies mentioning campaigns that extended Chola power across the Indian Ocean world.

title: Anbil Copper Plates of Sundara Chola (Parantaka II)
historical_significance: Crucial pre-Rajaraja Chola genealogy, bridging the gap between early medieval Chola records and the imperial age.
description: Copper plates issued by Sundara Chola Parantaka II from Anbil, documenting Chola lineage and a brahmadeya grant. Among the earliest securely dated plate sets linking the Chola revival to Rajaraja I's succession.

title: Tiruchchengodu Copper Plates of Uttama Chola
historical_significance: Confirms Uttama Chola's place in the succession between Sundara Chola and Rajaraja I, clarifying a debated phase of Chola history.
description: Copper-plate sasana of Uttama Chola Madhurantaka from Tiruchengode, granting land and recording the royal family. Essential for reconstructing the transition to Rajaraja I's reign at the turn of the eleventh century.

title: Tiruvarur Tyagaraja Temple Chola Inscription
historical_significance: Part of the vast Tiruvarur epigraphic corpus on temple ritual, land, and personnel under later Chola rulers.
description: Medieval Tamil inscription from the Tyagaraja temple at Tiruvarur, among numerous Chola-period records on devadasi endowments, land grants, and festival services under Parantaka I and successors.

title: Darasuram Airavatesvara Temple Chola Inscription
historical_significance: Documents late Chola temple foundation under Rajadhiraja Chola II, showing continuity of imperial temple culture before the dynasty's decline.
description: Foundation inscription of the Airavatesvara temple at Darasuram by Rajadhiraja Chola II (Rajarajacholadeva), dated 1166 CE. Details sculptural programmes, ritual staff, and endowments for the later Chola royal temple.

title: Manimangalam Inscription (Battle of Manimangalam)
historical_significance: Commemorates the pivotal battle between Pallava Narasimhavarman I and the Chalukya-Pallava struggle for Tondaimandalam.
description: Hero-stone and related epigraphic tradition associated with the Battle of Manimangalam (c. 642–652 CE), in which Pallava forces under Mahendravarman's son Narasimhavarman confronted the army of Pulakeshin II. A landmark in Pallava–Chalukya rivalry.

title: Mandagapattu Rock-cut Temple Inscription of Mahendravarman I
historical_significance: Earliest dated Pallava rock-cut temple inscription, inaugurating the dynasty's pioneering tradition of structural excavation without brick or mortar.
description: Sanskrit Grantha inscription in which king Vichitrachitta (Mahendravarman I) records the excavation of a Trimurti shrine at Mandagapattu 'without brick, timber, metal, or mortar' — the foundational charter of Pallava rock-cut architecture in Tamil Nadu.

title: Kanchipuram Kailasanatha Temple Inscription of Rajasimha
historical_significance: Associates the Kailasanatha temple with Pallava king Rajasimha (Narasimhavarman II), a masterpiece of early structural Pallava architecture.
description: Inscriptions on the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram attribute the complex to Rajasimha Pallava (Narasimhavarman II) and his queen Rangapataka. Among the most important Pallava records for dating the shift from rock-cut to structural stone temples.

title: Kudumiyanmalai Musical Inscription
historical_significance: Unique epigraphic evidence for ancient Tamil musical notation and the seven svaras, invaluable for music history.
description: Tamil inscription at the Kudumiyanmalai (Melakkoil) Siva temple with musical note names and sculptural directions. Associated with Pallava-period patronage under the Muttaraiyar chiefs of the Pudukkottai region.

title: Mamallapuram Panchapandava Mandapa Pallava Inscription
historical_significance: Part of the Mamallapuram epigraphic corpus under Mahendravarman I and Narasimhavarman I that documents the birth of Pallava rock-cut art.
description: Pallava-period Sanskrit inscription among the Mamallapuram rock-cut monuments, linked to the sculptural programme of the Panchapandava mandapa and the patronage of Narasimhavarman I (Mamalla), who expanded the port city's rock-cut complex.

title: Thiruketheeswaram Rajaraja I Pillar Inscription
historical_significance: Records Chola construction of Rajarajesvaram at Matottam and illuminates the administrative structure of Chola-ruled northern Sri Lanka.
description: Four-sided pillar inscription of Rajaraja Chola I from the Thiruketheeswaram area near Mannar, documenting the building of Rajarajesvaram and endowments for daily worship. A primary source for Chola occupation of the ancient port of Matottam.

title: Sangilikanadarawa Chola Inscription
historical_significance: Documents land and interest grants by a Chola army chief during the incorporation of Anuradhapura into the Chola province of Mummudi Cholamandalam.
description: Tamil inscription from Sangilikanadarawa near Anuradhapura, registering a grant of land and a monetary deposit at interest by the Chola commander Jayamurinatalavan to a local temple during the reign of Rajendra Chola I.

title: Pankuliya Tamil Buddhist Inscription (Anuradhapura)
historical_significance: Shows Tamil Buddhist patronage in ancient Anuradhapura, paralleling the Tamil householders' terrace record and early Indo-Lanka cultural exchange.
description: Tamil-Brahmi slab inscription of a Tamil merchant (Nanakuti) associated with a Buddhist establishment at Pankuliya in Anuradhapura. Part of the small but vital corpus of pre-Christian Tamil inscriptions in Sri Lanka.

title: Tiriyayi Tamil Buddhist Cave Inscription
historical_significance: Among the earliest Tamil inscriptions in eastern Sri Lanka, attesting Buddhist cave patronage by Tamil donors.
description: Tamil-Brahmi inscription in the cave monastery at Tiriyayi near Trincomalee, recording dedication by Tamil patrons. Belongs to the early historic layer of Tamil Buddhist activity on the island's eastern coast.

title: Polonnaruwa Shiva Devale Tamil Inscription
location: Polonnaruwa, North Central Province, Sri Lanka
historical_significance: Illustrates Chola Saiva temple foundations in Polonnaruwa after Rajaraja I's campaigns, renaming the city Jananathamangalam.
description: Tamil inscription from the Polonnaruwa Shiva Devale (built in Chola style during the occupation of Sri Lanka), reflecting imperial Saiva patronage and the transformation of Polonnaruwa into a Chola administrative centre.

title: Esalam Copper Plates of Rajendra Chola I
historical_significance: Preserves Rajendra Chola I's detailed conquest narratives and land grants, complementing Karandai and Thiruvalangadu plate sets.
description: Copper-plate grant of Rajendra Chola I dated to his 24th regnal year (1036 CE), with Sanskrit genealogy and Tamil dispositive sections. Records military achievements and endowments, among the major plate corpora of the imperial Chola period.

title: Tirupparankunram Subramanya Temple Pandya Inscription
historical_significance: Links the celebrated Murugan shrine near Madurai to medieval Pandya patronage after the post-Kalabhra revival.
description: Medieval Tamil inscription at the Subramanya temple on Tirupparankunram hill, recording Pandya-period grants and temple administration. The site also preserves much earlier rock-cut layers, but this record belongs to the restored Pandya kingdom.
